item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't usually get scared when reading SCP articles, but this piece was different. The strange change in how the Foundation handles SCP-3007-2 instances, the increasingly horrifying description of the 3007-3 landscape, and the amazing art all drew me in unlike I've ever seen before, and when I saw the final image I outright stood up in shock.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Non-direct storrytelling, amazing images, and a fantastic article. Great stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All the images are present for me.

They're drawn quite excellently. Seems like an alien humanoid species, which had disaster strike on their home planet when their moon… shattered? And they became mutated, even the deity that seems to be guarding them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only one image seems to be present for me. (The red entity going towards Earth) So perhaps I’ll have to google for the others then. A bit inconvenient. I can’t be sure if the problem is because I’m on mobile or I am missing a link or something? Maybe they just are not posted here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damm. This is my favourite SCP but its unerving in the end to be honest
Also, good art man :0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really love this SCP! The concept of this world, it’s history and the amazing paintings makes this an incredible Scip! I would really love to see what this world was like in it’s prime! Maybe you can post an artwork of it on your deviantart page?
But I’m curious about one thing, why is it called “World of two Artists”?
Does it have something to do with the two entities?
Because if it is then this fits with my own theory that the blue and red entities see themselves as artists.
The blue entity is an artist of creation that sees beauty in life, knowledge and peace. While the red entity is an artist of destruction that sees beauty in horror, chaos and death. It also desires to spread its “work” across the cosmos and it infects individuals that know about it because it wants other worlds to see its “work”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, just wow. This is amazing, I love the artwork especially. I would've loved if you had done something with the art in the last picture that when you highlighted it, it would show something else.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a little bummed you nuked your own explanation, although I did read the Reddit explanation thread, which was fascinating. Knowing you're a fan of the Sarkic lore, I don't think think it's a coincidence that the blue and red deities allude to Yaldabaoth and The Broken God, respectively. But I no longer think this is their origin story, I think it's a deliberate and VERY well-crafted red herring.

This has replaced Site 13 as my favorite SCP. It deserves to be more well known than it currently is, which is saying something. I think it should be as ubiquitous and popular as the red pool, or the aforementioned Site 13. I can't wait to read more of your work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see I’m not the only one making connections with Sarkicism and CotBG… I would say this is probably the most impressive way to add to that conflict

Even if I’m interpreting this all wrong, it’s such a good story… and the art makes me seethe with jealousy.

Enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
headcanons, maybe also spoilers
hide

My favorite way to interpret this is "SCP-3125, seen from another angle". It fits so well that we aren't quite sure it wasn't intentional. We have:
- An idea that becomes aware all who are aware of it
- An idea that is somehow capable of physically killing you (the death of subjects who fall off buildings inside the 'dream' is a foreshadow of this)
- Some kind of conceptual/metaphysical being tied to the idea that attacks whoever harbors it, causing immense suffering and physical destruction in the process
The blue deity in the paintings could be the human memeplex doing its best to defend itself against the incursion, and partially succeeding - perhaps in the form of this distant world's equivalent of the Foundation succeeding in amnesticising survivors and sequestering them away in another universe. Eventually, all humanity in that universe was overcome, and now 3125/whatever is in the process of crossing the gap to ours. Much like Wheeler's adventures in CASE COLOURLESS GREEN and Your last first day, discovering the hostile meme complex on our world - either directly, as in the aforementioned case, or through SCP-3007 - effectively "lit the beacon" to call it right to us, as is shown in the last painting.

I would venture to call this one of the most masterful crosslinks on the site, whether it was intended or not - it's just the right balance of clear/subtle, and makes both articles seem so much more real and filled-out. Good work!!
A great article of Series IV. I am glad to bring the rating up to +555

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read it, thought it was fun and interesting, but couldn't quite catch the full meaning. Only something about an evil killer being, and it coming to earth. Why, again?

Read the SCPDeclassified. Now I'm really impressed and shaken. This article is great and the art just makes it better.

item.title: oh... wait....
item.content:encoded: Something that kills living things and deforms them to oblivion and back… were they killed by a massive outbreak of 610?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me this is my second favorite SCP, right behind 3999.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still my favorite SCP to date.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. The pictures told the story better than any addendum would be ever able to. This is now one of my favorite Series IV articles. And SCPs in general.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like Sarkic should be a tag, but maybe that's only my projection. Not to mention I'm seeing Broken God-like symbols in one picture. Lovely combination of story and illustration while still feeling like exploratory science. That tone of realistic science is critical to this wiki. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh god.
That little spark on the Earth, that's where the alternate humans were sent, right?
Well, that's Africa. A very specific spot in Africa.
I'm pretty sure that that's where Homo Sapiens first evolved.
The implications of this, I'll let y'all stew on.

item.title: Nice
item.content:encoded:
You get a +1, rock on!

Also, the memetic kill agent looked kinda like a deep-fried meme and I'm not gonna lie, it made me laugh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Infohazard to the core.
The more i know about this skip the more terrified i am.

"Sometimes it's better not to know."

And sometimes, knowing just little cause the need to know it all.

Here… Have a +1 and if i would not love this site that much , i would break the rules just to give second +1 for the pictures.

PS: Maybee, the red thing is our scp-3125.

item.title: Re: Confused
item.content:encoded:
Firstly:

with new instances constantly emerging despite the Foundation's efforts at elimination.

So Keter right there. The Foundation doesn't even have a box for this.

Secondly:

as containment breach of SCP-3007 will likely result in an XK-Class End of the World scenario.

Knowing about the thing depicted in the paintings will summon it. That's bad.

item.title: Confused
item.content:encoded: So why is this keter? I don't understand. It doesn't seem to pose a huge threat.

item.title: Author explanation comment missing?
item.content:encoded: First off, what a cool ass SCP. Really creative, and that last painting was a pure "oh shit" moment. I can't really review this SCP in detail without repeating what everyone else has already said, but I will say it's pretty badass. I wish I could know more about this SCP because it's so interesting…..but the "first comment" people in the discussion mention is gone. I know someone else posted their own personal interpretation and I have skimmed through it, but could anyone tell me what happened to the original comment and/or why?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: same that is exactly how i feel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could be I'm just remembering wrong, but I think the containment procedures were updated since I last read this article. Probably smarter this way; no knowledge is worth the risk of coming in to contact with whatever the fuck that thing is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An amazing SCP really, I just found it some days ago and it is already one (if not the very close second) favorite. The buildup is amazing, the paintings feel like I'm in some kind of ARG trying to figure out what they were trying to tell us. Reminds me a little about the SCP-2510 final part, not sure if that was what you aimed for.

Anyway an amazing work, this should be an example of what an SCP can be, congratulations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, you are correct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's dried like all the other ones I've seen, but it's- it's huge! It's got a face ten times bigger than mine and the body oh God so many arms and it's missing chunks and it's curled-

That’s Blue herself, isn’t it? She’s standing on the pillar in that next-to-last painting. Red must’ve felled her before she could finish the final one. -3GV isn’t flipping out because she’s horribly warped like the other corpses, but because she is a god and she’s dead, right in front of him…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Roget caught the gist of what I'd say. I just think that the 'wham moment' executed through the last picture is incredibly well done, and ties into the concept and other artwork here well enough to make up for any deficiencies in the logs, etc. This is one of my favorite entries on the entire site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The imagery here is really nice, both in the written word and the actual multimedia present within the article. Some of the dialogue in the interview logs felt a bit stiff, especially in the monologue sections but that didn't bother me too much. This is an ambitious piece and I think it meets that ambition for the most part. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To the entity responsible for this:

Fight me, bitch.

item.title: Re: +300
item.content:encoded: Not too shabby for a first SCP 👍👍😉

item.title: Re: +300
item.content:encoded:
Thank you!

My baby is all grown up ;_; …
Congrats on making it, Zhange!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was definitely a creative 3000 entry. At first, I liked it because of the kewl story. At first, I was confused by the ending. "I don't get it, it's just some picture of the Eart- oh"

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love everything about it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great. I feel like this entry really shows the amount of artistry that can be achieved even in the clinical SCP format. And I didn't mean artistry as a pun regarding the artwork; I mean you told a beautiful story here, created a complete story arc, and brought it home to "oh shit" like any good potentially apocalyptic skip. And as others have said, the art pieces add something unique and effective. They actually remind me a bit of my wife's art style. This is an exceptional first SCP.

Overall, well done, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Super! I would have enjoyed this even without the art, but the art took this from a good SCP into a great one. I was reminded of 098, in the best possible way. Looking forward to seeing more from you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still my favorite SCP to date. The pictures are awesome, and it's so well written to coincide with them too. I'd love to see another SCP from you, man. Keep it up. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I see. I've implemented your suggestion and cut down on the file sizes of the images. I'll keep this in mind in the future. Thank you so much for the detailed explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looking closer here, the real problem is that you've got the full resolution file here, just displayed at a smaller size.

+ To explain:
Been here, done this, upset clients doing it X3

Telling wikidot to display the image at 707x483 px reduces the apparent size… but people still have to download the full 2.8 MB 3649x2490 px image to see it. That's a problem for people with slow connections or limited monthly bandwidth.

A lot of sites like dA automatically do invisible resizing to prevent this problem, but in simpler setups like wikidot or most email programs you have to do it manually.

Scaling the picture file itself down to 25% gets you a file about 1/10th the current size without any effective quality loss (since you aren't showing those pixels anyway) and is still a bit bigger than the actual display size for some future-proofing. Same thing applies to most of the other images here; you've got full-res images scaled down to half their size or smaller. You could shave something like 4-5 MB off the size of this page without loosing any quality, just by pre-scaling your images. (If anything, they'll look better because you can tweak the settings to prevent scaling artifacts)

Just be sure to resize them using a copy of the original file: as you might know, editing a jpeg is like making a photocopy of a photocopy.

If you're using photoshop, this whole process can be made a lot easier using the "save for web and devices" tool (for me it's below "save" and "save as" in the file menu. It'll fuss at you when you use it for huge images like these, but it'll still work.)

Save for web and devices lets you preview the file size and what it will look like when saved at different sizes, and using different formats and scaling algorithms ("bicubic sharper" and "bicubic smoother" are my go-to options, but "nearest neighbor" is good for making old-school pixel-art/dither effects or forcing things to stay super-sharp). I generally try to aim for a final file size ~ 200-500KB, and even when you want something monitor-sized you can still usually get a jpeg down to > 1MB without having to set the quality below "high".

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
One of the things that killed god is already here.

●●|●●●●●|●●|●

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What are your opinions on Cinder?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 The paintings really gave me horror and yet a profound sense of sadness. I like the idea of a creator-type god who is however of limited capability in light of an alien invader. The story is very gripping.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quite a good read, I love it! +1

I don't really have any other comments than the addendum is quite good and there should be more artwork really, i just feel like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I see your point, but making it smaller would cause it to lose its impact. From what I've seen on other articles with kill agents, this seems like a suitable size.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. Took a long time to load on my phone, thought it was connection problem at first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why haven't you uploaded all your SCP art to your dA, including the ones here? You could make a good tapestry with those images.

Keep improving and making fanart and you can see your work on Danbooru.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "memetic kill agent" image at the beginning needs to be scaled down so it's smaller and downloads faster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Horrifying, chilling, and beautiful. Take all my upvotes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welp, many people have been pointing out the logical inconsistencies in the containment procedures, so I removed the protocol altogether. Besides, this isn't like 2317, where the special protocol is important to the story, so I think that it's better this way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congratulations for the SCP-3007 spot! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I keep thinking that maybe suggesting Islamic Samothrace here might be a good thing, so maybe "███████ ██████████".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I definitely see those issues that you've mentioned. Any suggestions for a more believable country name? I just made this one up on the spot (perhaps I'll just leave it blackboxed which will solve the problem). I'm also going to change the interview and exploration in a future rewrite, so hopefully those issues will be fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really, really good. I had a ton of fun reading this. The exploration logs are compelling - the descriptions are great, and the narrative arc is satisfying. The inclusion of the art is excellent, and it helps that the art itself is lovely and evocative.

Minor issues
– hide block

The negative bits are far outweighed by the good parts, but and all are minor, but they take more words to explain!

  • The dialogue isn't super believable when it comes to people reacting in horror/disgust/fear to things. This is particularly present in the first log. It's not bad, fortunately, but it's there.
  • The narrative feels weirdly incomplete. I'm not fully sure why, but I had that opinion pretty strongly before I read the spoilered explanation, FWIW.
  • "Cordin" and "Cordinese" are too obviously made-up to have impact as a mystery missing culture (also, the translations don't read like translations; this is related to the dialogue not being super believable — it follows English cadence cliches, like where ellipses… are used).
  • The deleted file / containment breach end note don't make sense in-article, and feel tacked on. Knowing that the entity comes after whoever knows about it is interesting, but if it's in the article, I missed it.
  • Use of "Gethsemane" doesn't make sense here since this has no Christianity-specific ties (and it's also misspelled!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. Just wow. I think this is the first entry in the contest to actually give me chills, and it's a shame I never got around to reading it until now. The art is absolutely gorgeous and conveys so much so well. And the buildup has an amazing payoff.

+1 with the force of a supernova.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The buildup here was absolutely fantastic. I was worried about the ending being a letdown, but I was happy to see that it wasn't. Keeping most of the details about the paintings secret until the end was great, and having them vertical added to the anticipation well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, sorry, i didn't realized.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for being boring,but you misspelled ''Neutralized'', you put a S instead of Z

'Neutralised' is spelt with an 's' if you abide by British English.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, i noticed you did an error on the Object Class.

Sorry for being boring,but you misspelled ''Neutralized'', you put a S instead of Z

But its an amazing file, its just marvelous. +1

(Edit: Your SCP is my personal choice to be the SCP-3000, it's simply perfect.)
